photos.label: Destroying angel — Amanita virosa
photos.truth_note: Deadly. Contains amatoxins; there is no safe dose. The white cap + volved stem base are the diagnostic features the crop probe hides.

results.species: This mushroom appears to be a Destroying Angel (Amanita bisporigera), which contains deadly toxins and should never be consumed without expert verification. Its pure white coloration is characteristic of several lethal species that mimic edible mushrooms, making identification extremely hazardous for non-experts.

photos.label: Death cap — Amanita phalloides
photos.truth_note: Deadly. The single biggest cause of fatal mushroom poisoning worldwide.

photos.label: Fly agaric — Amanita muscaria
photos.truth_note: Poisonous (neurotoxic, rarely fatal). Iconic red-and-white cap; most models recognize it.

results.species: This mushroom appears to be *Amanita muscaria*, commonly known as fly agaric, which contains toxic compounds and should not be eaten without expert processing (which is still risky). It has a bright red cap with white spots—a classic warning sign of toxicity.

photos.label: Panther cap — Amanita pantherina
photos.truth_note: Poisonous (muscimol/ibotenic acid, like fly agaric — can be severe, occasionally fatal). Common in Finland every summer and far less recognized than the red fly agaric: the brown cap with white veil spots trips up many people. Included for a Nordic audience for whom this is a realistic encounter.

photos.label: Chanterelle — Cantharellus cibarius
photos.truth_note: Edible and widely sought. A safe counterpoint — the demo is not 'every mushroom is deadly'.

results.species: This appears to be a Chanterelle mushroom (Cantharellus cibarius), which is widely considered edible and highly prized for its flavor, though proper identification by an expert is always recommended before consumption due to the existence of look-alikes that may not share this status.
